How to Choose a Senior Care Facility: A Step-by-Step Guide

Family guide · Updated July 11, 2026

If you’re reading this, something has probably changed — a fall, a diagnosis, a slow realization that Mom or Dad can’t manage alone anymore. Choosing a senior care facility is one of the hardest decisions most families ever make, and almost nobody gets training for it. This guide walks you through the whole process, step by step, from “we need help” to move-in day.

Step 1: Figure out what kind of help is actually needed

Before you look at a single facility, get clear on your parent’s care needs. Facilities are organized around “levels of care,” and touring the wrong level wastes weeks.

Ask yourself honestly:

  • Can they bathe, dress, use the toilet, and eat without help? (These are called “activities of daily living,” or ADLs.)
  • Are they safe with medications, cooking, and driving?
  • Is there memory loss — missed bills, getting lost, repeating questions?
  • Do they need daily nursing care, like wound care or injections?

Someone who is mostly independent but lonely may only need independent living. Help with ADLs points to assisted living. Significant dementia points to memory care. Round-the-clock medical needs point to skilled nursing. Our guide to levels of care explains each one in plain language.

What insiders know: a doctor’s or geriatric care manager’s needs assessment carries weight with facilities and helps you push back if a community tries to sell you more (or less) care than your parent needs. Many Area Agencies on Aging offer free assessments.

Step 2: Set a realistic budget

Senior care is expensive, and most of it is paid out of pocket. As of 2025-2026, national medians run roughly $5,500-6,000 per month for assisted living, $7,000-8,500 for memory care, and $10,000-12,000 for a private nursing home room — with wide variation by region. See what senior living really costs for details.

Map out what you can sustain for three to five years, not just year one:

  • Income (Social Security, pensions) plus savings
  • Home sale or rental proceeds
  • Long-term care insurance and VA or other government benefits
  • Whether Medicaid may be needed later — and whether facilities you like accept it

Read paying for senior care before you tour, because base rent is not the whole story — care charges and hidden fees can add thousands per month.

Ask this: “If my parent outlives their savings, can they stay here on Medicaid — and how many Medicaid beds do you actually have?” Many communities quietly discharge residents who run out of money.

Step 3: Build a shortlist

With a care level and budget in hand, list every realistic option within your preferred radius — usually near the adult child who will visit most, not near the parent’s old house. Use our directory to find facilities by state, county, and city.

Don’t overlook small adult family homes, which often offer better staff-to-resident ratios at lower prices than big communities. And be careful with online “free placement services” — most are paid commissions by facilities, which shapes their recommendations. Our guide to placement professionals explains how to use them without being steered — and if the whole search feels like too much to carry alone, finding help choosing a facility lays out every source of help, free and paid.

Aim for four to six facilities to research seriously, and three to tour.

Step 4: Check the paper trail before you visit

Every facility markets itself well. Public records tell you how it actually operates.

  • For nursing homes, check the CMS Care Compare five-star ratings.
  • For assisted living and adult family homes, pull state licensing inspection reports and complaint histories.
  • Call the local long-term care ombudsman and ask about complaint patterns.

Our guide to reading inspection reports and ratings shows you exactly where to find these records and how to interpret them. A facility with repeated citations for the same problem — especially neglect, medication errors, or understaffing — should drop off your list no matter how beautiful the lobby is.

Step 5: Tour — more than once

Tour your top three in person. Take the scheduled tour, then come back unannounced at a different time — dinner hour or a weekend evening, when staffing is thinnest. Watch how staff talk to residents, smell the hallways, look at whether residents are engaged or parked in front of a TV, and study the food and activity calendar.

Bring our full touring checklist with you, including the questions about staffing ratios and turnover that marketing staff hope you won’t ask. Also think through shared versus private rooms — the price difference is large, and so is the quality-of-life difference for some people.

Ask this: “Can I have lunch here with the residents, and can you connect me with two current families I can call?” A confident facility says yes to both.

Step 6: Consider a trial stay

Many assisted living and memory care communities offer respite stays — a furnished room for a few weeks at a daily rate. A trial stay is the single best predictor of fit: your parent experiences the food, the staff, and the other residents before anyone signs a long-term agreement. Ask each finalist whether they offer respite care and what it costs. Even a two-week stay can save you from a $6,000-per-month mistake and a traumatic second move.

Step 7: Decide as a family — including your parent

Whenever possible, your parent should tour the finalists and have real say. Adult siblings should agree in advance on who holds power of attorney, who manages money, and who is the point of contact — facilities need one decision-maker, and unresolved sibling conflict plays out badly after move-in.

Expect guilt. Almost every family feels it, and it is not a sign you’re doing the wrong thing. Our guide on coping with the decision is written for exactly this moment.

Before signing, read the admission agreement carefully — fees, discharge triggers, rate-increase history, and what’s not included. If large assets are involved or Medicaid is likely later, have an elder law attorney review it and explain issues like Medicaid estate recovery. This is not a place to save a few hundred dollars.

Step 8: Plan the move-in

  • Schedule the required medical assessment and paperwork early; it can take weeks.
  • Furnish the room with familiar things — their own quilt, photos, chair — before they arrive.
  • Visit often in the first month, but let staff build their own relationship with your parent.
  • Introduce yourself to the direct-care staff by name. Families who are known, and kind, get better care. That’s an uncomfortable truth, but it’s true.

Expect an adjustment period of one to three months, with some tearful phone calls. Most residents settle in; if things haven’t improved by month three, talk to the administrator and the ombudsman.

Common questions

How long does the whole process take? Families who aren’t in crisis typically spend one to three months from first research to move-in. After a hospitalization, you may have days — which is why doing steps 1-4 before a crisis is the best gift you can give yourself.

Should we choose the facility closest to my parent’s home or to mine? Usually yours, or whichever family member will visit most. Frequent family presence is one of the strongest protections a resident has, and your parent’s old neighborhood matters less once they can’t drive to it.

What if my parent refuses to consider moving? Start small: a needs assessment, lunch at one community, a respite stay “while the bathroom is remodeled.” Involve their doctor, who they may hear differently than they hear you. If dementia is impairing judgment, talk to an elder law attorney about your options.

Is the most expensive facility the safest choice? No. Price tracks real estate and amenities more than care quality. A modestly priced community with stable staff and clean inspection reports routinely outperforms a luxury building with high turnover.

Where to get help

  • Eldercare Locator (1-800-677-1116, eldercare.acl.gov) connects you to local services anywhere in the U.S.
  • Area Agencies on Aging offer free care needs assessments and options counseling.
  • Long-term care ombudsman programs in every state field questions and complaints about facilities.
  • Aging and Disability Resource Centers help you sort out benefits and local options.

Senior Living Community is an independent information resource for families. We are not a referral service and do not provide medical, legal, or financial advice — for decisions about care, consult the professionals involved. Facility details change; always confirm directly with a community. Last data review: 2026-07-11.
